revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[hu:Akaruhime]][[ja:阿加流比売神]][[ko:아카루히메]][[zh:阿加流比賣神]]
{{Infobox deity|gender={{ill|female|qid=Q6581072|1=Female (gender)}}|consort={{ill|Amenohiboko|qid=Q713100|1=Amenohiboko}}; {{ill|Q17216052|qid=Q17216052|1=Tsunuga Arashito}}}}


'''Akaruhime no Kami''' (阿加流比売神) is a {{ill|Goddess|en|Goddess|qid=Q205985|lt=Goddess|1=Goddess}} appearing in [[Japanese mythology]].

== Overview ==
In the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|qid=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, she is the wife of {{ill|Amenohiboko|ja|天之日矛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hiboko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hiboko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノヒボコ}}, the prince of {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|qid=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, her name is not mentioned, but the story is paralleled in the tale of {{ill|Tsunuga Arashito|ja|都怒我阿羅斯等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunuga Arashito|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunuga Arashito|qq=}}, the prince of [[:en:Gaya confederacy|Gaya]], who chases a maiden. Although the names of the country, husband, and woman differ between the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, the narratives are notably similar.
The name "Akaru" is interpreted as "bright," and combined with "hime" (princess), it is believed to mean "a woman of beautiful, radiant complexion."<ref>Shincho Nihon Koten Shusei: Kojiki</ref> Another interpretation holds that she is a spirit embodied in a red {{ill|carnelian|ja|瑪瑙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/carnelian|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/carnelian|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to メノウ|1=Agate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q123314}} gemstone, symbolizing the rising sun.
== Mythological Accounts ==
=== Kojiki ===
In the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|qid=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, the story appears in the records of {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|qid=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}}:
A woman napped near a marsh named Agunuma (阿具奴摩) in Silla, and sunlight, shining like a rainbow, touched her body. She became pregnant and gave birth to a red jewel. A man observing the scene pleaded for the jewel, received it, and carried it with him always. One day, when carrying food on an ox, he encountered {{ill|Amenohiboko|ja|天之日矛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hiboko|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Hiboko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アメノヒボコ}}, who misunderstood him as intending to kill the ox and tried to imprison him. The man offered the red jewel to be pardoned. Taking the jewel home, Ame-no-Hiboko placed it on his bed, where it transformed into a beautiful maiden.
Ame-no-Hiboko married the maiden, who served delicious meals every day. However, after he insulted her one day in arrogance, she fled by boat to {{ill|Namba|en|Namba|qid=Q1075406|lt=Naniwa|1=Namba}} and became enshrined at the {{ill|Himegoso Shrine|ja|比売碁曾神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himegoso Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himegoso Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, identified as Akaruhime-no-Kami.
=== Nihon Shoki ===
In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, under the record of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|qid=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}}:
{{ill|Tsunuga Arashito|ja|都怒我阿羅斯等|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunuga Arashito|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsunuga Arashito|qq=}} lost his ox while traveling. Tracking it, he discovered that a villager had killed and eaten the ox, suspecting it was meant for food. As compensation, the villager gave him a sacred white stone. When Tsunuga Arashito brought the stone home and placed it on his bed, it turned into a beautiful maiden.
He tried to approach her, but she vanished, heading eastward. His wife revealed that the maiden had gone to Naniwa and became the deity of Himegoso Shrine. She was also worshipped at {{ill|Himegosho Shrine|ja|比売語曽神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himegosho Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himegosho Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in {{ill|Kunisaki District|ja|国東郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisaki District|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kunisaki District|1=Kunisaki district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10926861}}, {{ill|Toyo Province|en|Toyo Province|qid=Q1205043|lt=Toyokuni|1=Toyo Province}}.
=== Settsu no Kuni Fudoki (Lost Text) ===
The {{ill|Settsu Province|en|Settsu Province|qid=Q864163|lt=Settsu Province|1=Settsu Province}} Fudoki fragment also contains a similar account:
During the time of {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|qid=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}}, a goddess fled from her husband in Silla and lived on Hime Island (伊波比の比売島) in {{ill|Tsukushi Province|en|Tsukushi Province|qid=Q1206461|lt=Chikushi Province|1=Tsukushi Province}}. Fearing discovery, she departed for an island in Naniwa, naming it Hime Island after her former home.
== Interpretation ==
The birth story of Akaruhime-no-Kami is an example of an {{ill|oviparous myth|ja|卵生神話|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oviparous myth|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/oviparous myth|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, similar to legends found across East Asia, such as the stories of {{ill|Dongmyeong of Goguryeo|en|Dongmyeong of Goguryeo|qid=Q378502|lt=Jumong|1=Dongmyeong of Goguryeo}}, the founder of {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|qid=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo|1=Goguryeo}}, {{ill|Hyeokgeose of Silla|en|Hyeokgeose of Silla|qid=Q484898|lt=Hyeokgeose of Silla|1=Hyeokgeose of Silla}}, and {{ill|Suro of Geumgwan Gaya|en|Suro of Geumgwan Gaya|qid=Q496836|lt=Suro of Geumgwan Gaya|1=Suro of Geumgwan Gaya}} of {{ill|Geumgwan Gaya|ja|金官国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geumgwan Gaya|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Geumgwan Gaya|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
The {{ill|Himegoso Shrine|ja|比売許曽神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himegoso Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himegoso Shrine|1=Himekoso Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11547445}} in {{ill|Higashinari-ku, Osaka|en|Higashinari-ku, Osaka|qid=Q985701|lt=Higashinari Ward, Osaka|1=Higashinari-ku, Osaka}}, Osaka, is believed to correspond to the shrine mentioned in the Kojiki, though its current main deity is {{ill|Shitateruhime|ja|下照比売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitateruhime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シタテルヒメ}}, the daughter of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|en|Ōkuninushi|qid=Q276944|lt=Ōkuninushi|1=Ōkuninushi}}.
The Himejima Shrine (姫嶋神社) in {{ill|Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|en|Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka|qid=Q985765|lt=Nishiyodogawa Ward, Osaka|1=Nishiyodogawa-ku, Osaka}} enshrines Akaruhime-no-Kami alongside the {{ill|Sumiyoshi sanjin|en|Sumiyoshi sanjin|qid=Q1114013|lt=Sumiyoshi sanjin|1=Sumiyoshi sanjin}}. Additionally, Akaruhime-no-Kami is honored at the {{ill|Akarihime Shrine|ja|赤留比売命神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akarihime Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akarihime Shrine|1=Akaruhimenomikoto Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q117848511}} (Sanjuppo Shrine) in {{ill|Hirano-ku, Osaka|en|Hirano-ku, Osaka|qid=Q861256|lt=Hirano Ward|1=Hirano-ku, Osaka}}, Osaka.
The Himegosho Shrine (比売語曽社) in {{ill|Himeshima, Ōita|en|Himeshima, Ōita|qid=Q1351003|lt=Himeshima Village|1=Himeshima, Ōita}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|Ōita Prefecture|qid=Q133924|lt=Ōita Prefecture|1=Ōita Prefecture}}, is identified as the "Himegosho Shrine" from the Nihon Shoki.
== Genealogy ==
'''Family Tree of Ame-no-Hiboko'''

{{familytree|border=0|01||| 01=[[Tajimanomatao]] (多遅摩之俣尾)|qq=}}
